chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents and Solutions

notes.section: 9.1 What Are Solute, Solvent, and Solution?

content.text: A mixture is formed when two or more substances combine. Mixtures are classified into two categories based on how evenly the components are distributed: uniform mixtures and non-uniform mixtures. Uniform mixtures have components evenly distributed throughout, so the composition is the same throughout the mixture. Non-uniform mixtures have components that are not evenly distributed, so different parts of the mixture may have different compositions.

content.title: Examples of Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Mixtures
content.text: Uniform mixtures: Sugar dissolved in water (sweetness is uniform throughout), salt dissolved in water (saltiness is uniform throughout), Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) where every sip tastes the same. Non-uniform mixtures: Chalk powder mixed with water (chalk particles are visible), sand and water (sand particles settle separately), sawdust and water (sawdust floats separately).

Every sip of homemade ORS tastes the same because sugar and salt are uniformly distributed throughout the water. The uniform distribution ensures consistent taste and concentration throughout the mixture, no matter which portion is consumed.

A solution is a uniform mixture formed when one substance dissolves in another. A solution consists of two main components: the solute and the solvent. The solute is the substance that is dissolved (usually present in smaller amount), and the solvent is the substance in which the solute dissolves (usually present in larger amount).

Key principle: Solute + Solvent = Solution. The solute must completely dissolve in the solvent, creating a uniform mixture where the solute particles are evenly distributed throughout the solvent at the molecular/atomic level.

When a solid dissolves in a liquid, identification is straightforward: the solid is the solute and the liquid is the solvent. However, when two liquids mix, the component present in smaller quantity is considered the solute, while the component present in larger quantity is considered the solvent.

content.title: Solute and Solvent Examples
content.text: Sugar solution: Sugar (solute) dissolves in water (solvent). Salt solution: Salt (solute) dissolves in water (solvent). Gulab jamun syrup: Although sugar is the major component, water is still considered the solvent because it is the dissolving medium that enables sugar's dissolution. Air: Nitrogen is the solvent (78% of air), while oxygen (21%), argon, and carbon dioxide are the solutes.

Not all solutions are liquid. Gases can also form solutions with other gases. Air is the most common example of a gaseous solution, comprising approximately 78% nitrogen (the solvent), 21% oxygen (a solute), and about 1% other gases including argon, carbon dioxide, and trace gases (also solutes). Just as in liquid solutions, air is a uniform mixture where the components are evenly distributed throughout.

Gaseous solutions demonstrate that the solvent-solute relationship depends on the relative quantities present, not the state of matter. In air, the component present in the greatest amount (nitrogen) is the solvent, regardless of the fact that all components are gases.

notes.section: 9.2 How Much Solute Can a Fixed Amount of Solvent Dissolve?

content.title: Investigating Salt Dissolution (Activity 9.1)
steps: Fill a glass tumbler halfway with water
Add one spoonful of salt and stir until completely dissolved
Gradually add more salt spoonfuls, stirring after each addition
Observe when salt stops dissolving completely
Note the point when undissolved salt begins to settle at the bottom
Record observations for each addition

content.text: When salt is first added to water, it dissolves completely, forming a uniform solution. As more salt is added and stirred, it continues to dissolve completely for several additions. However, after adding a certain amount, a stage is reached where the added salt no longer dissolves completely. Instead, solid salt particles settle at the bottom despite stirring. This indicates that the water has reached its maximum capacity to dissolve salt at that temperature.

Unsaturated solution: A solution in which more solute can be dissolved at a given temperature. At this stage, the solution can still dissolve additional solute. Saturated solution: A solution in which the maximum amount of solute has been dissolved and no more solute can dissolve at that particular temperature. The solute begins to settle as undissolved solid at the bottom.

topics.topic: Concentration, Solubility, and Dilute vs. Concentrated

content.text: Concentration is the amount of solute present in a fixed quantity of solution (or solvent). Based on concentration, solutions are classified as dilute or concentrated. A dilute solution contains a small amount of solute in a given quantity of solvent, while a concentrated solution contains a large amount of solute in the same quantity of solvent. These are relative terms—a solution that is dilute compared to another may be concentrated compared to a third.

content.title: Concentration Comparison
content.text: One spoonful of salt in 100 mL of water produces a dilute solution. Two spoonfuls of salt in 100 mL of water produces a more concentrated solution. Four spoonfuls of salt in 100 mL of water produces an even more concentrated solution. All three are unsaturated at room temperature; they differ only in concentration.

Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a fixed quantity of solvent (typically 100 mL) at a given temperature. Solubility is a definite property of a substance, while concentration describes a particular solution. Solubility tells us the theoretical limit of dissolution; concentration tells us how close a specific solution is to that limit.

content.title: Temperature Effect on Baking Soda Solubility (Activity 9.2)
steps: Take 50 mL water at room temperature (approximately 20°C)
Add baking soda spoonful by spoonful until some remains undissolved
Heat the contents to 50°C while stirring
Observe: The undissolved baking soda dissolves
Add more baking soda until some again remains undissolved at 50°C
Heat further to 70°C while stirring
Observe: The previously undissolved baking soda dissolves
Record observations and compare amounts dissolved at each temperature

content.text: Temperature significantly affects the solubility of most solids in liquids. As temperature increases, the solubility of most solids generally increases. This means that at higher temperatures, more solute can dissolve in the same amount of solvent. For example, hot water dissolves more sugar, salt, or baking soda than cold water of the same volume.

Critical observation: A saturated solution at a lower temperature becomes an unsaturated solution when heated. If a solution is saturated at 20°C and you heat it to 50°C, it is no longer saturated at the new temperature—additional solute can dissolve. Conversely, if you cool a saturated solution, it may remain saturated or even become supersaturated (with excess solute precipitating out).

This temperature dependence is important for practical applications: hot water dissolves solutes more easily than cold water, which is why sugar dissolves faster in hot tea than in cold juice. Industrial processes often use heat to dissolve and then cool to crystallize desired substances.

topics.topic: Gas Dissolution and Reverse Temperature Effect

content.text: Unlike solids, which generally become more soluble with increasing temperature, gases show the opposite behavior: gas solubility in liquids DECREASES as temperature increases. Many important gases dissolve in water, including oxygen (O₂), carbon dioxide (CO₂), and nitrogen (N₂). Although gases dissolve in much smaller amounts compared to solids, these dissolved gases are crucial for life.

Key difference: Temperature effect is opposite for gases vs. solids. Solids: Higher temperature → Greater solubility. Gases: Higher temperature → Lower solubility. More gas dissolves in cold water than in warm water at the same pressure.

Oxygen dissolves in water only to a small extent, yet this dissolved oxygen is essential for aquatic life. Fish, aquatic plants, and all other aquatic organisms depend on dissolved oxygen for respiration. Cold water can hold more dissolved oxygen than warm water, which is why aquatic ecosystems thrive better in colder waters. When water warms up, oxygen becomes less soluble and escapes from the solution. This is why aquatic organisms may struggle in warm, stagnant water with low oxygen content.

Gases in water form uniform solutions just like solids do, with the gas particles evenly distributed throughout the liquid. This is why carbonated drinks taste the same throughout—the dissolved carbon dioxide is uniformly distributed.

notes.section: 9.4 Why Do Objects Float or Sink in Water?

content.text: Some objects float in water while others sink. Common observations: wooden sticks float on water; iron nails sink. Oil poured into water floats on the surface. During rice washing, husk particles float while rice grains sink. While many assume that floating objects are simply 'lighter' and sinking objects are 'heavier,' this intuition is not precise. What truly determines whether an object floats or sinks is a property called density, which compares the mass of the object to its volume.

content.title: Floating and Sinking Examples
content.text: A large wooden block may be much heavier than a small iron ball in terms of mass, yet the wooden block floats while the iron ball sinks. This is because density (mass per unit volume) differs. A pencil floats in oil but sinks in water—not because the pencil changes, but because density relationships differ.

Density is defined as the mass of a substance per unit volume. Mathematically: Density = Mass / Volume. In simpler terms, density describes how much matter is packed into a given space. A dense forest has trees closely packed together; a less dense forest has trees far apart. Similarly, a dense substance has more mass packed into the same volume compared to a less dense substance.

Key principle: Density is an intensive property, meaning it does not depend on the size or shape of the object. A small block of iron has the same density as a large block of iron. Density depends on the substance itself and on conditions like temperature and pressure.

Units of density depend on the units chosen for mass and volume. The SI unit is kilogram per cubic metre (kg/m³). For liquids and solids in laboratory settings, common units are gram per millilitre (g/mL) and gram per cubic centimetre (g/cm³). These are convenient because water at room temperature has a density very close to 1 g/mL, making calculations simple.

content.title: Density Calculation
content.text: An aluminium block has mass 27 g and volume 10 cm³. Density = 27 g / 10 cm³ = 2.7 g/cm³. This means aluminium is 2.7 times as dense as water (which has density 1 g/cm³). The relative density of aluminium with respect to water is 2.7 (a unitless number).

content.title: Measuring Mass with Digital Balance (Activity 9.3)
steps: Switch ON the digital weighing balance
Observe initial reading—should show zero
If not zero, press tare/reset button to zero the display
Place a clean, dry watch glass on the balance pan
Note the reading of the watch glass alone
Press tare/reset button to return to zero (taring subtracts watch glass weight)
Place the solid object on the watch glass
Note the displayed reading—this is the mass of the object (e.g., 16.400 g)

content.text: Mass is the quantity of matter present in an object or substance. It is measured using a balance, with units of grams (g) or kilograms (kg). The taring function of a digital balance is crucial: it subtracts the weight of the container or support, ensuring only the mass of the object itself is measured. For liquids, the same process is used, but with a beaker instead of a watch glass.

Important distinction: Mass and weight are different. Mass is the amount of matter (in grams or kilograms). Weight is the force exerted by gravity (in newtons). Most balances measure weight but display the result in mass units. For our purposes at Earth's surface, this distinction is usually not critical.

Volume is the space occupied by an object or substance. For liquids, volume is conveniently measured in litres (L) or millilitres (mL), where 1 L = 1000 mL = 1 dm³ and 1 mL = 1 cm³. The primary instrument for measuring liquid volume in the laboratory is a measuring cylinder, a narrow transparent container with volume markings on the side.

content.title: Reading a Measuring Cylinder (Activity 9.4)
steps: Observe the total volume marked on the cylinder (e.g., 100 mL capacity)
Identify the larger division marks (major markings)
Count the smaller divisions between major marks
Determine the volume represented by one small division
For 100 mL cylinder: If 10 divisions exist between 10 mL and 20 mL marks, each division = 1 mL
The smallest volume readable is determined by this smallest division

content.text: Measuring cylinders are designed to be narrow and tall (not wide and short like beakers) to maximize accuracy. The narrow design ensures that even small volume changes result in noticeable height changes on the scale, improving reading accuracy. Different cylinder sizes have different minimum readable volumes: 0.1 mL for 10-25 mL cylinders, 1 mL for 100 mL cylinders, 2 mL for 250 mL cylinders, and 5 mL for 500 mL cylinders.

content.title: Measuring Water in a Cylinder (Activity 9.5)
steps: Place clean, dry measuring cylinder on a flat, level surface
Pour water slowly into the cylinder to the desired mark
Observe the water surface—it forms a curved surface called the meniscus
For clear liquids, read the mark at the BOTTOM of the meniscus
For coloured liquids, read the mark at the TOP of the meniscus
Position eyes at level with the meniscus bottom for accurate reading
Adjust with dropper if needed to reach the exact mark
Transfer water to the required container

content.text: The meniscus is important: it is the curved surface formed by the liquid at the container edges. For water (clear liquid), the meniscus curves downward (concave), so we read at the lowest point. This accounts for the liquid's interaction with the glass walls.
For solid objects with regular shapes (cubes, cuboids, cylinders), volume can be calculated using geometric formulas. For irregular-shaped objects, the displacement method must be used.

content.title: Volume of Regular Solids (Activity 9.6)
steps: Select objects with regular shapes (notebook, dice, shoebox, block)
Measure length (l), width (w), and height (h) using a ruler or scale
Apply formula: Volume = l × w × h
Example: Notebook with l=25 cm, w=18 cm, h=2 cm
Volume = 25 × 18 × 2 = 900 cm³
Record measurements and calculations

content.title: Volume of Irregular Solids by Displacement (Activity 9.7)
steps: Fill a measuring cylinder with water to a known volume (e.g., 50 mL)
Record this initial volume
Tie an irregular object (stone, key) with a thread
Slowly lower the object into the measuring cylinder
Record the final water level (e.g., 55 mL)
Volume of object = Final level - Initial level = 55 - 50 = 5 mL = 5 cm³
The object displaces water equal to its own volume

content.text: The displacement method works because when an object is submerged, it pushes water aside by a volume equal to its own volume. By measuring the water level change, we determine the object's volume. This method works for any object that doesn't absorb water, including metals, rocks, and most common materials.

Once mass and volume are measured, density can be calculated using the formula: Density = Mass / Volume. For example, if a stone has mass 16.4 g and volume 5 cm³, its density is 16.4 / 5 = 3.28 g/cm³.

content.title: Density Calculation Steps
content.text: Measure mass using balance: 16.4 g. Measure volume using displacement method: 5 cm³. Calculate: Density = 16.4 g ÷ 5 cm³ = 3.28 g/cm³. This tells us the stone is 3.28 times as dense as water.

Relative density compares a substance's density to water's density. Relative density = Density of substance / Density of water. Since water's density is approximately 1 g/cm³, relative density is simply the numerical value of density in g/cm³. For example, if aluminium has density 2.7 g/cm³, its relative density is 2.7.

Generally, density decreases with heating and increases with cooling. This occurs because heating causes particles to move faster and spread apart, increasing volume without changing mass. Since Density = Mass/Volume, when volume increases with heating (mass constant), density decreases.

content.title: Temperature Effects on Density
content.text: Hot air has lower density than cool air, so hot air rises (used in hot air balloons). Metal expands when heated, so its density decreases slightly. Water behaves anomalously: it has maximum density at 4°C, not at lower temperatures.

Water's special property: Water's density is highest at 4°C (1.00 g/mL). As temperature drops below 4°C or rises above 4°C, density decreases. This is why ice (solid water at 0°C) has lower density than liquid water and floats. This anomaly is crucial for aquatic life: ice floats on top, insulating water below and keeping fish alive in winter.

Pressure affects density differently depending on the state of matter. For gases, increasing pressure decreases volume (particles compress closer), so density increases. For liquids, pressure has a small effect because they are nearly incompressible—volume changes very little even under significant pressure, so density change is minimal. For solids, pressure has negligible effect on density because particles are already very tightly packed.

content.title: Pressure Effects
content.text: Gas: Compressing air into a tire increases pressure and density. Liquid: Ocean water experiences enormous pressure at great depths, but density increases only slightly. Solid: Pressure on a steel block causes negligible density change.

An object floats in a liquid if its density is less than the liquid's density. An object sinks if its density is greater than the liquid's density. An object neither floats nor sinks if its density equals the liquid's density (neutral buoyancy). This is because objects less dense than the displacing liquid experience a buoyant force greater than their weight.

content.title: Floating and Sinking Examples
content.text: Oil (density ~0.9 g/cm³) floats on water (density 1.0 g/cm³). Iron (density ~7.9 g/cm³) sinks in water. Ice (density ~0.92 g/cm³ at 0°C) floats on water. A raw egg (density slightly > 1 g/cm³) sinks in fresh water but floats in salt water (denser due to dissolved salt).

The shape and structure of objects can affect buoyancy independent of density. A flat wooden board floats despite wood being denser in isolated pieces. This is because the large surface area and air pockets distribute the weight, reducing effective density. Similarly, ships made of steel (denser than water) float due to hollow construction that traps air, lowering the overall average density of the ship plus air combination.

glossary.definition: A uniform mixture formed when one substance (solute) dissolves completely in another (solvent), creating a homogeneous blend.
The substance that dissolves in a solvent. Usually present in smaller amount. In liquid-liquid mixtures, the component present in smaller quantity is the solute.
The substance in which a solute dissolves. Usually present in larger amount. Water is called the universal solvent because many substances dissolve in it.
A combination of two or more substances. Can be uniform (solution) or non-uniform (components not evenly distributed).
A mixture in which components are evenly distributed throughout, having the same composition throughout. Also called a solution.
A mixture in which components are not evenly distributed. Different parts may have different compositions. Components may be visible.
A solution in which the maximum amount of solute has been dissolved at a given temperature. No more solute can dissolve at that temperature.
A solution in which more solute can still be dissolved at a given temperature. The solution has not reached its dissolution limit.
The amount of solute present in a fixed quantity of solution or solvent. Described as dilute (little solute) or concentrated (much solute).
The maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a fixed quantity of solvent (typically 100 mL) at a particular temperature. A definite property of a substance.
A solution containing a relatively small amount of solute in a given quantity of solvent. A relative term depending on comparison.
A solution containing a relatively large amount of solute in a given quantity of solvent. A relative term depending on comparison.
The process of a solute dissolving in a solvent to form a solution. Solid solute particles separate and disperse throughout the solvent.
The mass of a substance per unit volume. Formula: Density = Mass / Volume. SI unit: kg/m³. Common unit for liquids: g/mL or g/cm³.
The quantity of matter present in an object or substance. Measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg) using a balance.
The space occupied by an object or substance. Measured in cubic metres (m³), litres (L), or millilitres (mL). 1 L = 1000 mL = 1 dm³.
The ratio of a substance's density to water's density. A unitless number. Relative density = Density of substance / Density of water.
The curved surface formed by a liquid in a measuring cylinder or similar container. For water, the meniscus is concave (curves downward).
A method to measure the volume of irregular solid objects by measuring how much water they displace when submerged.
A narrow, transparent cylindrical container with volume markings used to measure liquid volume accurately.
An instrument used to measure the mass of objects. Digital balances display mass directly in grams or kilograms.
The upward force exerted by a fluid (liquid or gas) on an object submerged or floating in it. Related to density differences.
To remain on or near the surface of a liquid. Objects with density less than the liquid's density float.
To descend through a liquid to the bottom. Objects with density greater than the liquid's density sink.
A solution formed when gases mix with each other or with liquids. Air is a common gaseous solution with nitrogen as solvent.

summary_points: A solution is a uniform mixture where one substance (solute) completely dissolves in another (solvent), creating an evenly distributed composition throughout. Every part of a solution has identical composition and properties.

In solid-liquid solutions, the solid is the solute and the liquid is the solvent. In liquid-liquid solutions, the component present in larger amount is the solvent, and the component present in smaller amount is the solute. In gases, the most abundant component is the solvent.

An unsaturated solution can dissolve more solute at a given temperature. A saturated solution has reached the maximum solute dissolution capacity at that temperature—no more can dissolve without changing conditions.

Solubility (the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in fixed solvent quantity) changes with temperature. For most solids: solubility increases with temperature. For gases: solubility decreases with temperature. A saturated solution becomes unsaturated when heated.

Density is mass per unit volume (Density = Mass / Volume). It is an intensive property independent of object size or shape but dependent on temperature and pressure. Different substances have different densities, which determines floating and sinking.

Mass is measured using balances in grams or kilograms. Volume is measured using measuring cylinders for liquids (in mL) or by displacement method for irregular solids. Density calculations require accurate measurement of both mass and volume.

Objects float if their density is less than the surrounding liquid's density. Objects sink if their density is greater. The effective density of objects can be altered by shape and structure—ships float despite steel being denser than water because of their hollow design.

Temperature affects density: most substances decrease in density with heating (volume increases, mass constant). Water shows anomalous behavior: maximum density at 4°C; ice floats because solid water is less dense than liquid water, which is crucial for aquatic life survival.

Pressure has different effects on density by state: gases compress under pressure (density increases), liquids are nearly incompressible (minor density change), solids are very incompressible (negligible density change). This reflects particle packing differences between states.

Measuring cylinders are designed narrow and tall to maximize accuracy: narrow design amplifies small volume changes into noticeable height changes on the scale. Reading technique requires positioning eyes level with the meniscus bottom for accurate measurement of liquid volumes.
